titel: Technical issues with the Swedish Consumer Agency's website have caused an old news item about a product recall to be sent out again.
html:
Cookies:

We use necessary cookies to ensure the website functions properly for you. We also want to use cookies to improve the website through statistics. Do you consent to this? Yes, I agree No, I decline Learn more about cookies.

Technical issues with the Swedish Consumer Agency's website – an old news item about a product recall has been sent out again. News Published: June 9, 2026

A news item regarding a trampoline recall from January 2025 was incorrectly sent to subscribers today. The Swedish Consumer Agency apologizes for this error. The incorrect notification concerned a trampoline that, due to safety flaws, could cause injury. The Swedish Consumer Agency apologizes for the incident. Troubleshooting is underway to prevent this from happening again. More news:

Consumers are vulnerable when purchasing cosmetic procedures. Many companies advertising procedures and treatments violate the rules. News June 23, 2026

Legislative change makes it easier to cancel online purchases. On June 19th, the law is changing to make it easier to cancel online purchases. News June 8, 2026

Easier contact with electricity companies after review. The Swedish Consumer Agency reviewed 35 electricity companies this spring. The purpose was to check if they display email addresses and phone numbers for customer service. News May 28, 2026
tldr: The Swedish Consumer Agency (Konsumentverket) accidentally sent out an old warning about a dangerous trampoline to its subscribers due to a technical error on their website.
lattlast:
The Swedish Consumer Agency, the authority responsible for ensuring consumer rights and protection when making purchases, has experienced technical issues with its website. Due to these problems, an old news item was incorrectly sent out to people who follow their updates.

The old news item concerned a trampoline that was deemed unsafe and had already been recalled in early 2025. The Consumer Agency apologizes for the re-issuance of this information and is currently working to fix the error so that it does not happen again.

It's important to note that the warning about the trampoline is outdated, even though it recently appeared in the newsletter.
